questions.text: Which derivative of benzene shows maximum reactivity in electrophilic substitution reactions?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Benzoic acid (C6H5COOH) is a crystalline organic compound. Benzoic acid is reactive in electrophilic substitution reactions due to the presence of the benzene ring in its structure. The benzene ring in benzoic acid is electron-rich and can undergo substitution reactions with electrophiles.

Activating groups increase the reactivity of electrophilic substitutions, whereas deactivating groups decrease it.Activating groups are (from most activating to least): -NH2, -NR2 > -OH, -OR> -NHCOR> -CH3 and other alkyl groups, where R is an alkyl group.Deactivating groups are (from most deactivating to least): -NO2, -CF3> -COR (COR can be both carboxylic acids or aldehydes), -CN, -CO2R, -SO3H > Halogens.Among the options, only B describes benzene with an activating group so, it is correct. All other options describe deactivating groups as one can see from the lists mentioned.
Benzaldehyde (C6H5CHO) is an aromatic aldehyde and is also reactive in electrophilic substitution reactions due to the presence of the benzene ring in its structure.
Nitrobenzene (C6H5NO2) is an aromatic compound that is highly reactive in electrophilic substitution reactions due to the presence of the nitro group (-NO2) attached to the benzene ring.

questions.explanation: Activating groups increase the reactivity of electrophilic substitutions, whereas deactivating groups decrease it.Activating groups are (from most activating to least): -NH2, -NR2 > -OH, -OR> -NHCOR> -CH3 and other alkyl groups, where R is an alkyl group.Deactivating groups are (from most deactivating to least): -NO2, -CF3> -COR (COR can be both carboxylic acids or aldehydes), -CN, -CO2R, -SO3H > Halogens.Among the options, only B describes benzene with an activating group so, it is correct. All other options describe deactivating groups as one can see from the lists mentioned.

However, in actuality, this is not so, as Benzene is highly stable and forms substitution compounds easily.

Di-vinyl acetylene is incorrect because acetylene undergoes trimerization to form benzene in this reaction.
Neoprene is incorrect because acetylene undergoes trimerization to form benzene in this reaction.
This is the correct answer. Acetylene undergoes trimerization to form benzene under the specified conditions with the help of an organo-nickel catalyst.

questions.explanation: When acetylene is passed under pressure over an organo-nickel catalyst at 70°C, it undergoes a trimerization reaction where three molecules of acetylene combine to form one molecule of benzene. The organo-nickel catalyst facilitates the cyclization process, allowing this transformation to occur.The other options are incorrect because they do not align with the trimerization reaction described in the question.

questions.text: Chlorine in Vinyl chloride is not very reactive because
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: C−Cl bond develops partial double bonds
options.explanation: In vinyl chloride, the C−Cl bond exhibits some degree of double bond character due to resonance. This is because the carbon atom involved in the C−Cl bond is sp2 hybridized and forms a pi (π) bond with the chlorine atom. The pi bond contributes to the stability of the molecule and reduces the reactivity of the C−Cl bond compared to a typical single bond.

Resonance refers to the delocalization of electrons within a molecule. While resonance does occur in vinyl chloride, it is more directly related to the partial double bond character of the C−Cl bond, as explained in option (a).

options.text: Sp2 hybridized carbon has more acidic character than sp3 hybridized carbon
options.explanation: This statement is generally not true. The acidity of a carbon atom is primarily determined by the stability of the conjugate base that forms after losing a proton. The sp2 hybridized carbon in vinyl chloride does not significantly affect its acidity in the context of the given question.

This option is not correct because the main reason for the low reactivity of chlorine in vinyl chloride is the development of partial double bond character (option a), rather than resonance (option b) or the acidic character of the carbon atom (option c).

questions.explanation: The chlorine in vinyl chloride is not very reactive because the C−Cl bond develops partial double bonds due to the pi bond formed between the sp2 hybridized carbon and the chlorine atom. This partial double bond character reduces the reactivity of the C−Cl bond.

This option is incorrect as it includes aryl halides that are not the correct reactants for the Friedel-Crafts alkylation reaction to produce toluene. The correct reactant is methyl chloride.

The other options either represent incorrect reactants or different products that can be formed through alternative reactions.

questions.text: What are Kekule's structures?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: False! The Kekulé structures are two resonance structures that were proposed by Friedrich August Kekulé to represent the bonding in benzene. In the Kekulé structures, benzene is depicted as alternating single and double bonds between carbon atoms. One structure shows three double bonds and three single bonds, while the other structure shows the opposite pattern. However, these structures alone cannot fully explain the stability and properties of benzene. In reality, benzene exhibits a phenomenon called resonance, where the electrons are delocalized and spread out over the entire ring. This delocalization gives benzene its unique stability and reactivity. So, while the Kekulé structures are a helpful representation, they are not the complete picture of benzene's bonding.

questions.text: Aniline is a derivative of benzene which contains
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: An imino group (-NH-) is not present in aniline. It consists of a nitrogen atom bonded to a carbon atom, which is not found in aniline.
The correct option. Aniline contains an amino group (-NH2), which is a nitrogen atom bonded to two hydrogen atoms.
An amide group (-CONH2) is not present in aniline. It consists of a carbonyl group (C=O) bonded to a nitrogen atom, followed by a hydrogen atom.
A nitro group (-NO2) is not present in aniline. It consists of a nitrogen atom bonded to two oxygen atoms (-NO2), and it is not part of the molecular structure of aniline.

questions.explanation: Aniline is a derivative of benzene in which one of the hydrogen atoms of the benzene ring is replaced by an amino group (-NH2). This amino group, consisting of a nitrogen atom bonded to two hydrogen atoms, is what distinguishes aniline from benzene. Therefore, the correct answer is the amino group. The other options, imino group, amide group, and nitro group, do not match the chemical composition of aniline and are incorrect.

questions.text: Which one of the following is correct structure of benzene?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Tetrahedral is not the correct structure for benzene. Benzene has a planar hexagonal structure with delocalized pi electrons.

This is the correct structure of benzene. Benzene consists of a planar ring of six carbon atoms with delocalized electrons, making the carbon-carbon bonds equal in length and intermediate between single and double bonds.
Hexagonal irregular is not the correct structure for benzene. Benzene has a symmetrical planar hexagonal structure with delocalized pi electrons.
Trigonal planar is not the correct structure for benzene. Benzene has a planar hexagonal structure with delocalized pi electrons, not trigonal planar geometry.

questions.explanation: The correct structure of benzene is a planar ring of six carbon atoms with delocalized electrons. This delocalization gives all carbon-carbon bonds equal length and intermediate bond strength between single and double bonds. The other options are incorrect as they do not represent the unique hexagonal planar structure of benzene.

questions.text: Kolbe's electrolytic method is used to prepare ethyne. Which of the following salts of carboxylic acid is used for this purpose?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Sodium acetate is a salt of acetic acid, not the specific salt used in Kolbe's electrolytic method to prepare ethyne.
Sodium succinate is a salt of succinic acid, not the salt used in Kolbe's electrolytic method to prepare ethyne.
Sodium oxalate is a salt of oxalic acid, not the specific salt used in Kolbe's electrolytic method.
Potassium maleate is the correct salt of carboxylic acid used in Kolbe's electrolytic method to prepare ethyne. The electrolysis of potassium maleate produces ethyne.

questions.explanation: Potassium maleate, a salt of maleic acid, is the specific salt used in Kolbe's electrolytic method to prepare ethyne. The electrolysis of potassium maleate results in the production of ethyne. The other options, sodium acetate, sodium succinate, and sodium oxalate, are salts of different carboxylic acids and are not the salts used in the Kolbe's electrolytic method.

questions.text: Catalytic hydrogenation of alkenes exhibits:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.explanation: Catalytic hydrogenation of alkenes exhibits addition reactions. Addition reactions in an unsaturated compound mean a compound that has a double bond can undergo addition reactions. The addition reactions can be classified into three main categories. Electrophilic addition: In these types of reactions, a species, that accepts an electron pair, attacks the electron-rich site of the double bond between carbon atoms. The reaction examples include the reactions of alkenes with hydrogen halides to give alkyl halides. Nucleophilic addition: In these types of reactions, species that have electron pairs, donate to form a chemical bond. These reactions usually occur in compounds having a carbonyl group like aldehydes or ketones. Free-radical addition: In these types of reactions, non-polar molecules form bonds with unsaturated compounds. These reactions occur when free radicals are formed. Free radicals are species that have unpaired valence electrons.

questions.text: The unhybridized p orbitals of benzene are present in the form of _ ?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Between the C-C bonds of benzene
options.explanation: The unhybridized p orbitals of benzene are not located between the carbon-carbon (C-C) bonds but rather above and below the benzene ring.

options.text: Above and below the carbon bonds
options.explanation: This option is similar to the correct answer, as the unhybridized p orbitals are indeed located above and below the carbon bonds in the form of a cloud.

options.text: In the form of cloud above and below the benzene ring
options.explanation: Benzene is a cyclic molecule in which all of the ring atoms are sp2-hybridized. This allows the pi electrons to be delocalized in molecular orbitals that extend all the way around the ring, above and below the plane. The unhybridized p orbital lie above and below the benzene ring.
This option is incorrect as the correct answer is C) in the form of a cloud above and below the benzene ring.

questions.explanation: Benzene is a cyclic molecule in which all of the ring atoms are sp2-hybridized. This allows the pi electrons to be delocalized in molecular orbitals that extend all the way around the ring, above and below the plane. The unhybridized p orbital lie above and below the benzene ring.

It have 3 fused rings.Therefore it is incorrect.
It have 3 fused rings.Therefore it is incorrect.

Benzene ring is isolated in the compound Triphenylmethane, which has three phenyl groups (-C6H5) attached to a central carbon atom. The other compounds mentioned in the options, naphthalene, phenanthrene, and anthracene, are all polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) that contain two or more fused benzene rings. Naphthalene, phenanthrene, and anthracene all contain two or more benzene rings fused together. Triphenylmethane, on the other hand, consists of a central carbon atom bonded to three phenyl groups, each of which contains a benzene ring.

Hexene is a different compound that belongs to the alkene family and is not formed as a result of the polymerization of acetylene.

questions.explanation: This option is correct since polymer is formed by combination of monomers. At 873 K when acetylene is passed through a red hot iron tube the molecules of acetylene undergo chain polymerization and form benzene.

questions.text: How many resonance structures of benzene are possible?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: . Kekulé Structures (2 structures):These are the two most well-known resonance forms of benzene. Each has alternating double and single bonds arranged differently:In one, the double bonds are between carbons 1–2, 3–4, and 5–6In the other, they are between 2–3, 4–5, and 6–1These two structures represent the delocalization of π electrons, and the real structure of benzene is a hybrid of both.2. Dewar Structures (3 structures):These are less common but still valid contributing forms. Each Dewar structure involves a different set of three conjugated double bonds and two cross-ring single bonds, giving a kind of strained tricyclic form.Although Dewar structures contribute less to the resonance hybrid (because they are higher in energy and less stable than Kekulé forms), they are still valid resonance contributors in the complete quantum mechanical model of benzene.
B is incorrect since it states that benzene has three resonating structures which is not the case.
C is incorrect since it states that benzene has six resonating structures which is not the case.
D is incorrect since it states that benzene has four resonating structures which is not the case.

questions.explanation: . Kekulé Structures (2 structures):These are the two most well-known resonance forms of benzene. Each has alternating double and single bonds arranged differently:In one, the double bonds are between carbons 1–2, 3–4, and 5–6In the other, they are between 2–3, 4–5, and 6–1These two structures represent the delocalization of π electrons, and the real structure of benzene is a hybrid of both.2. Dewar Structures (3 structures):These are less common but still valid contributing forms. Each Dewar structure involves a different set of three conjugated double bonds and two cross-ring single bonds, giving a kind of strained tricyclic form.Although Dewar structures contribute less to the resonance hybrid (because they are higher in energy and less stable than Kekulé forms), they are still valid resonance contributors in the complete quantum mechanical model of benzene.

questions.text: Ethyne has which hybridization?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: sp³ Hybridizationsp³ hybridization happens when a carbon atom is bonded to four atoms/groups, like in methane (CH₄).It leads to tetrahedral geometry with 109.5° bond angles.In ethyne, carbon is only bonded to two atoms, NOT four, so sp³ is incorrect here.

sp² Hybridizationsp² occurs when a carbon is bonded to three atoms/groups, like in ethene (C₂H₄) (double bond).Trigonal planar geometry with 120° bond angles.Since ethyne has a triple bond and linear shape, sp² does not apply here.

Ethyne (C₂H₂) is a linear molecule with a triple bond between the two carbon atoms. Each carbon atom forms:One sigma (σ) bond with hydrogenOne sigma (σ) bond and two pi (π) bonds with the other carbon atom (forming a triple bond)Hybridization of carbon in ethyne:Each carbon atom uses sp hybrid orbitals to form sigma bonds.The remaining two unhybridized p orbitals on each carbon form the two pi bonds.Geometry:Linear geometryBond angle: 180°Final Answer: sp hybridization

sp²d Hybridizationsp²d is not typical for carbon — carbon cannot expand its octet, so d-orbital participation does not occur in its hybridization.This type of hybridization is sometimes discussed for elements in the 3rd period and beyond, but carbon (2nd period) doesn't use d-orbitals.So sp²d is completely incorrect for carbon and especially for ethyne.

questions.explanation: In ethyne (C₂H₂), each carbon atom is attached to two atoms (one hydrogen and one carbon).So, each carbon forms two sigma bonds and has two unhybridized p orbitals left to form pi bonds in the triple bond.Linear shape with 180° bond angle, which is characteristic of sp hybridization.

questions.explanation: Alkanes cannot be oxidized; rather they can be oxidized only when burned in the presence of O2 to give carbon dioxide and water. Only option A is an alkane.

questions.text: Which of the following compounds will form a hydrocarbon on reaction with Grignard reagent?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Grignard reagent (RMgX) reacts with C2H5OH to form a hydrocarbon according to the following equation. C2H5OH + RMgX → RH + C2H5OMgX. Hence, A is the answer.
B is an ester and an ester reacts first with a Grignard reagent to form a ketone, which reacts further to give a tertiary alcohol.
C is a ketone and reacting a Grignard reagent with a ketone gives a tertiary alcohol.
D is an aldehyde and reacting a Grignard reagent with an aldehyde (except HCHO) gives a secondary alcohol.

questions.explanation: A Grignard reagent reacts with compounds containing active H atom, such as water, ammonia, amines, alcohols, alkynes etc., to form a hydrocarbon. It does not form hydrocarbon with ethanal and propanone. CH3CH2OH will form a hydrocarbon on reaction with Grignard's reagent as on reaction with Grignard's reagent −OH group will get replaced by the R− group of RMgX leading to the formation of hydrocarbon.

questions.text: The resonance energy of benzene is :

options.explanation: Difference between amount of heat actually released and that calculated on the basis of the Kekule’s structure is called the ‘Resonance energy’ of the compound.The resonance energy of benzene is 150.5 kJ/mol as: Calculated ΔH = (3 x -119.5) =-358.5 kJ/moleObserved ΔH = -208 kJ/moleDifference = 150.5 kJ/mole Hence, this is not the correct option.

questions.text: Which catalyst is used to oxidize benzene rings?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: It is used in electrophilic subsitution reaction.Hence it is incorrect.

V2O5 (vanadium pentoxide) is another catalyst that can be used for oxidizing benzene rings. It is particularly employed in a process called the Baeyer-Villiger oxidation, which involves the conversion of ketones into esters or lactones. Although not specifically for oxidizing benzene rings, it can be utilized to introduce functional groups adjacent to a benzene ring. The Baeyer-Villiger oxidation using V2O5 typically involves the following steps: 1. Formation of a peroxycarboxylic acid: The ketone substrate is reacted with a peroxide, such as hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), in the presence of V2O5 as a catalyst. This reaction results in the formation of a peroxycarboxylic acid intermediate. 2. Migration of the oxygen: The oxygen of the peroxycarboxylic acid migrates to an adjacent carbon atom, resulting in the formation of an ester or lactone. This migration occurs through a reaction mechanism known as a Baeyer-Villiger rearrangement. The V2O5 catalyst facilitates the oxidation process by providing a surface for the reaction to occur, thereby increasing the reaction rate. The exact mechanism by which V2O5 catalyzes this oxidation is not fully understood, but it is believed to involve the activation and transfer of oxygen species. It's important to note that the Baeyer-Villiger oxidation using V2O5 is not commonly used for the direct oxidation of benzene rings. The more common method for oxidizing benzene rings is through nitration, as mentioned in the previous response. The most commonly used catalyst for the oxidation of benzene rings is a mixture of concentrated nitric acid (HNO3) and concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4). This combination is known as a "mixed acid" or "nitration mixture." The process of oxidizing benzene rings using a nitration mixture is called nitration. The reaction involves the substitution of one or more hydrogen atoms on the benzene ring with nitro groups (-NO2). The reaction is typically carried out under carefully controlled conditions, such as low temperatures and with the addition of a diluent or solvent, to prevent excessive side reactions. It's important to note that the nitration of benzene is a highly exothermic reaction and requires caution due to the potential hazards associated with the use of concentrated acids and the possibility of explosive reactions. Therefore, it is typically carried out in specialized industrial settings or under controlled laboratory conditions. This is a factual recall question. The catalytic oxidation of benzene is done using doped V2O5 as catalyst.
It is used in oxidation of aldehydes therefore incorrect.
It is used in hydrogenation.therefore incorrect.

This reaction results in the formation of a peroxycarboxylic acid intermediate. 2. The more common method for oxidizing benzene rings is through nitration, as mentioned in the previous response. This is a factual recall question. The catalytic oxidation of benzene is done using doped V2O5 as catalyst. (Source PCTB book 2, page 186)

questions.text: Kekule's formula shows benzene _.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: a) Highly saturatedKekule's formula for benzene does not represent it as highly saturated. In fact, Kekule's original formula showed benzene as a cyclic compound with alternating single and double bonds, which implied high unsaturation.

b) Highly unsaturatedCorrect Answer: b) Highly unsaturatedKekule's formula for benzene is highly unsaturated. It represents benzene as a cyclic compound with alternating single and double bonds between carbon atoms. However, we now know that benzene is a flat, hexagonal molecule with a delocalized π-bond system, and it does not have alternating single and double bonds as shown in Kekule's formula.

c) Gives substitution reactionsKekule's formula does not specifically describe the reactivity of benzene. It represents the structural arrangement of atoms but does not directly relate to the reactions that benzene undergoes.

d) Gives elimination reactionsKekule's formula does not indicate that benzene gives elimination reactions. Elimination reactions involve the removal of atoms or groups from a molecule to form a double bond or multiple bonds between two adjacent atoms, but this is not related to the structure represented in Kekule's formula.

questions.explanation: Option B. Kekule formula with three double bonds demands a high degree of unsaturation from benzene while usually it exhibits a saturated character.

questions.text: Which of the following compounds react slower than benzene in electrophilic substitution reaction?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Yes, nitrobenzene generally reacts slower than benzene in electrophilic substitution reactions. This is due to the presence of the nitro (-NO2) group in nitrobenzene, which is an electron-withdrawing group. The nitro group has a strong electron-withdrawing effect due to the presence of the electronegative oxygen and nitrogen atoms. This electron-withdrawing effect decreases the electron density on the benzene ring, making it less reactive towards electrophiles. As a result, the rate of electrophilic substitution reactions is generally slower for nitrobenzene compared to benzene. Additionally, the nitro group in nitrobenzene can also direct the incoming electrophile to different positions on the benzene ring compared to benzene itself. Nitrobenzene is a meta-directing compound, meaning that it tends to direct electrophiles to the meta position relative to the nitro group. This can further affect the reactivity and regioselectivity of electrophilic substitution reactions on the benzene ring. Overall, the presence of the nitro group in nitrobenzene reduces the reactivity and alters the regioselectivity of electrophilic substitution reactions compared to benzene. An electrophilic substitution reaction is a chemical process in which a compound's functional group is replaced by an electrophile. In this case, the displaced group is a hydrogen atom. The derivatives of benzene which have activating groups attached push electron density towards the ring, making it more susceptible to electrophilic attack and consequently, more rapid reactions. Similarly, the derivatives of benzene which have a deactivating group attached withdraw electron density away from the ring, making it less susceptible to electrophilic attack and consequently, slower reactions. Nitrobenzene is an example of such a derivative which is less reactive than benzene because the nitro group is a deactivating group (meta-directing). Therefore, option A is the correct answer.

No, phenol generally reacts faster than benzene in electrophilic substitution reactions. The presence of the hydroxyl (-OH) group in phenol enhances its reactivity compared to benzene. The hydroxyl group in phenol is electron-donating due to the presence of the oxygen atom, which increases the electron density on the benzene ring. This electron-donating effect makes phenol more reactive towards electrophiles compared to benzene. The increased electron density facilitates the formation of a sigma complex with the electrophile, which is the first step in many electrophilic substitution reactions. In addition to enhancing reactivity, the hydroxyl group in phenol also has an ortho-para directing effect on the incoming electrophile. It tends to direct electrophiles to the ortho and para positions relative to the hydroxyl group. This regioselectivity can result in the formation of specific products in electrophilic substitution reactions on the benzene ring of phenol. Therefore, due to the electron-donating nature of the hydroxyl group, phenol generally reacts faster than benzene in electrophilic substitution reactions and exhibits specific regioselectivity. Option B is incorrect because in phenol, the OH group is an activating group. (ortho-para directing).

Aniline generally reacts faster than benzene in electrophilic substitution reactions. The amino group (-NH2) in aniline is an electron-donating group, which increases the electron density on the benzene ring and enhances its reactivity towards electrophiles. The amino group donates electrons to the benzene ring through resonance, making aniline more nucleophilic than benzene. This increased electron density facilitates the formation of a sigma complex with the electrophile, making the reaction more favorable and faster compared to benzene. In addition to its electron-donating effect, the amino group in aniline also has an ortho-para directing effect on the incoming electrophile. It tends to direct electrophiles to the ortho and para positions relative to the amino group. This regioselectivity can result in the formation of specific products in electrophilic substitution reactions on the benzene ring of aniline. However, it is worth noting that the amino group in aniline is also a weakly deactivating group due to the nitrogen's higher electronegativity. This means that while aniline is more reactive than benzene, it may be slightly less reactive compared to other electron-donating groups, such as phenol. Nonetheless, aniline is still generally more reactive than benzene in electrophilic substitution reactions due to the presence of the amino group. Option C is incorrect because in aniline, the NH3 group is an activating group. (ortho-para directing).

No, toluene generally reacts faster than benzene in electrophilic substitution reactions. Toluene is a derivative of benzene in which a methyl group (-CH3) is attached to the benzene ring. The presence of the methyl group in toluene increases its reactivity compared to benzene. The methyl group is an electron-donating group that donates electron density to the benzene ring through resonance. This electron-donating effect enhances the electron density on the ring, making it more nucleophilic and thus more reactive towards electrophiles. The increased electron density facilitates the formation of a sigma complex with the electrophile, making the reaction faster compared to benzene. Moreover, the methyl group in toluene has a slightly ortho-para directing effect on the incoming electrophile. It tends to direct electrophiles to the ortho and para positions relative to the methyl group. This regioselectivity can result in the formation of specific products in electrophilic substitution reactions on the benzene ring of toluene. Therefore, due to the electron-donating nature of the methyl group, toluene generally reacts faster than benzene in electrophilic substitution reactions and exhibits specific regioselectivity.No, toluene generally reacts faster than benzene in electrophilic substitution reactions. Toluene is a derivative of benzene in which a methyl group (-CH3) is attached to the benzene ring. The presence of the methyl group in toluene increases its reactivity compared to benzene. The methyl group is an electron-donating group that donates electron density to the benzene ring through resonance. This electron-donating effect enhances the electron density on the ring, making it more nucleophilic and thus more reactive towards electrophiles. The increased electron density facilitates the formation of a sigma complex with the electrophile, making the reaction faster compared to benzene. Moreover, the methyl group in toluene has a slightly ortho-para directing effect on the incoming electrophile. It tends to direct electrophiles to the ortho and para positions relative to the methyl group. questions.text: Which one of the following is a powerful electrophile used to attack on the electrons of a benzene ring?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: FeCl2 is not an electrophile itself but acts as a Lewis acid, capable of accepting an electron pair. It does not directly attack the electrons of a benzene ring.
FeCl4- is not an electrophile itself but acts as a Lewis acid, capable of accepting an electron pair. It does not directly attack the electrons of a benzene ring.

The most powerful electrophile used to attack the electrons of a benzene ring is Cl+ (chlorine cation). It is a strong electrophile that can undergo electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions with benzene, forming chlorinated derivatives.
Cl2 (chlorine gas) is not an electrophile on its own. It is relatively unreactive towards benzene and does not directly attack the electrons of the benzene ring.

questions.explanation: When benzene reacts with a powerful electrophile like Cl+ (chlorine cation), it undergoes electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions where the positively charged carbon of Cl+ attacks the electron-rich benzene ring. This leads to the formation of chlorinated derivatives of benzene. In contrast, FeCl2 and FeCl4- act as Lewis acids, accepting electron pairs but not directly attacking benzene. Cl2 (chlorine gas) is unreactive towards benzene and does not serve as an electrophile in attacking the benzene ring.

questions.text: Which of the following reaction does acetylene give due to its acidic hydrogens?

options.explanation: Acetylene does not readily undergo ozonolysis.
Acetylene does not undergo hydroxylation reactions directly.

Acetylene (C2H2) possesses two hydrogen atoms bonded to carbon atoms in its structure that exhibit acidic properties. These hydrogen atoms can be readily removed by strong bases, resulting in the formation of acetylides.
Kolbe's electrolysis is a reaction that occurs with carboxylic acids, not with acetylene.

questions.explanation: Acetylene (C2H2) possesses two hydrogen atoms bonded to carbon atoms in its structure that exhibit acidic properties. These hydrogen atoms can be readily removed by strong bases, resulting in the formation of acetylides.

questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The electron donating group such as the alkyl group donates electrons to the benzene ring and hence increases the density. The electron density at the ortho and para positions of the benzene ring is increased by the alkyl group through hyperconjugation.

An atom or group that makes the benzene molecule less reactive by removing electron density from the ring acts as a deactivating group. Contrarily, Activating groups serve as ortho‐para directors when they are attached to a benzene ring, meaning that they direct an incoming electrophile to the ortho or para positions and thus, increase the electron density.

questions.text: All of the following methods explain stability of benzene EXCEPT:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is a valid explanation for the stability of benzene. Benzene exhibits resonance, where electrons are delocalized across the ring, resulting in a more stable structure. The resonance energy contributes to the overall stability of benzene. Hence, this is not the correct answer.

The resonance method is essentially a way of representing the delocalization of electrons in a molecule, and it is closely related to the concept of resonance energy. It is a valid method for explaining the stability of benzene. Hence, this is not the correct answer.

Crystal field theory primarily deals with the electronic structure and bonding in transition metal complexes, not aromatic hydrocarbons like benzene. It is not a suitable method for explaining the stability of benzene. Hence, this is the correct answer.

The atomic orbital treatment, particularly in the context of molecular orbital theory, can be used to explain the delocalization of pi electrons in benzene. This treatment is consistent with the idea of resonance and contributes to the stability of benzene. Hence, this is not the correct option.

questions.explanation: Crystal field theory primarily deals with the electronic structure and bonding in transition metal complexes, not aromatic hydrocarbons like benzene. It is not a suitable method for explaining the stability of benzene. Resonance energy and resonance method are both valid explanations for the stability of benzene due to the delocalization of electrons in the ring. The atomic orbital treatment, although related to resonance, is not the primary method for explaining the stability of benzene.

questions.text: Reduction of benzene is presence of Ni gives:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Reduction of benzene in the presence of nickel (Ni) results in the formation of cyclohexane. This reaction is known as catalytic hydrogenation, which involves the addition of hydrogen gas (H2) in the presence of a catalyst, usually a transition metal such as Ni, Pt, or Pd.

Heterocyclic compounds contain at least one atom other than carbon in the ring structure, such as nitrogen, oxygen, or sulfur. The reduction of benzene in the presence of nickel does not typically result in the formation of heterocyclic compounds.

Alicyclic compounds are cyclic hydrocarbons that do not possess aromatic properties. While cyclohexane is an alicyclic compound, not all alicyclic compounds are formed by the reduction of benzene in the presence of nickel.

Unsaturated aliphatic compounds contain carbon-carbon double or triple bonds. The reduction of benzene in the presence of nickel results in the saturation of the benzene ring, converting it into a saturated compound (cyclohexane). Therefore, the product is not an unsaturated aliphatic compound.

questions.explanation: Reduction of benzene in the presence of nickel (Ni) results in the formation of cyclohexane. This reaction is known as catalytic hydrogenation, which involves the addition of hydrogen gas (H2) in the presence of a catalyst, usually a transition metal such as Ni, Pt, or Pd.

questions.text: With the help of Cyclic polymerization, Benzene can be prepared from
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Alkane cannot be polymerized to form benzene because it does not have any double bonds. A double bond is required for polymerization to occur.
Alkene can only be polymerized to form linear molecules, not cyclic molecules like benzene. This is because alkenes have only one double bond, and two double bonds are needed to form a benzene ring.
Alkyne is the correct option. Benzene can be prepared from alkynes through a process called cyclic polymerization where three alkyne molecules are joined together to form a benzene molecule.
Alkyl is not a molecule, so it cannot be polymerized at all. An alkyl group is a substituent that can be attached to a molecule, but it is not a molecule itself.

questions.explanation: Benzene can be prepared from alkynes through a process called cyclic polymerization. In cyclic polymerization, three alkyne molecules are joined together to form a benzene molecule. The reaction is shown below:3C2H2 ? C6H6Alkanes do not have double bonds required for polymerization. Alkenes can only form linear molecules, not cyclic like benzene. Alkyl is not a molecule and cannot be polymerized.

questions.text: How do meta directing groups affect the electrophilic substitution of benzene ring?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: Increase electron density at the ortho and para positions
options.explanation: Increasing electron density at the ortho and para positions is incorrect because meta-directing groups have the opposite effect and decrease the electron density at these positions.

options.text: Decrease electron density at the ortho and para positions
options.explanation: The meta groups deactivate the ring, by the inductive effect, in the presence of an electronegative atom, that withdraws the electrons away from the ring.
Since the conditions are unfavourable for an electrophile to bind, we can assume benzene to have undergone a loss of reactivity. This option is incorrect.

options.text: Both Options B and C are correct
options.explanation: The meta groups deactivate the ring, by the inductive effect, in the presence of an electronegative atom, that withdraws the electrons away from the ring. As the electron is withdrawn from the ring, by the meta group, it leaves the carbons, at the ortho and para positions, with a positive charge, which is unfavourable for the electrophile, so the electrophile attacks carbon at the meta positions. Since the conditions are unfavourable for an electrophile to bind, we can assume benzene to have undergone a loss of reactivity.

questions.explanation: The meta groups deactivate the ring, by the inductive effect, in the presence of an electronegative atom, that withdraws the electrons away from the ring. As the electron is withdrawn from the ring, by the meta group, it leaves the carbons, at the ortho and para positions, with a positive charge, which is unfavorable for the electrophile, so the electrophile attacks the carbon at the meta positions. Since the conditions are unfavourable for an electrophile to bind, we can assume benzene to have undergone a loss of reactivity.

questions.text: Activation of the benzene ring in aniline can be decreased by treating with:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Dilute hydrochloric acid (HCl) is not effective in decreasing the activation of the benzene ring in aniline.

Ethyl alcohol (C2H5OH) does not decrease the activation of the benzene ring in aniline. It does not introduce any electron-withdrawing groups to the benzene ring and does not have an acylating effect.

Acetic acid (CH3COOH) alone does not decrease the activation of the benzene ring in aniline. While acetic acid can be used as a solvent in some reactions, it does not have a direct acylating effect on the amino group of aniline.

To decrease the activation of the benzene ring in aniline, the correct option is to treat it with acetyl chloride (CH3COCl), which leads to the acylation of the amino group and the introduction of an electron-withdrawing group to the benzene ring.

questions.explanation: In aniline, the amino group is an electron-releasing group. It activates the benzene ring towards an electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction. The activating effect of the amino group is reduced by acetylations. The acetyl group being electron withdrawing attracts the lone pair of electrons of the N-atoms towards itself.

questions.text: Benzene is extra stable because of _?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Benzene is extra stable because of something called aromaticity. Aromaticity refers to a special type of stability that certain compounds, like benzene, possess. In benzene, the electrons in the carbon-carbon bonds are delocalized, meaning they are spread out over the entire ring structure. This electron delocalization creates a stable and balanced electron cloud, making benzene more stable compared to other compounds. It's this unique stability that gives benzene its special properties and makes it an important building block in many chemical reactions.

Options A, B, and C individually do not fully explain the concept of aromaticity and its role in benzene's stability.

questions.text: The ratio of σ to π bonds in benzene is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In benzene, a cyclic aromatic compound, the ratio of σ (sigma) to π (pi) bonds is 1:3.Benzene has a molecular formula of C6H6 and consists of a ring of six carbon atoms with alternating single (σ) and double (π) bonds. Each carbon atom in the benzene ring is sp2 hybridized, meaning it forms three sigma bonds with three neighboring carbon atoms. The remaining p-orbital on each carbon atom overlaps sideways with the p-orbitals of the adjacent carbon atoms, forming a π bond.The structure of benzene can be represented by a resonance hybrid, where the π electrons are delocalized over the entire ring rather than being localized between specific carbon atoms. This delocalization of π electrons gives benzene its stability and unique properties.So, in benzene, there are six sigma bonds (σ) and 18 pi bonds (3 π bonds per double bond). Therefore, the ratio of σ to π bonds in benzene is 1:3.The structure of benzene reveals that it has 3 C-C double bonds, hence it has three π bonds for each double bond that it makes. It also possesses 6 C-C single bonds and 6 C-H single bonds, making a total of 12 σ bonds.Ratio of σ:π = 12:3 = 4:1Therefore option A is the correct answer. OIP.kJ8bRcHGdSByWKiFdeWugwHaHa (474×474) (bing.com)

questions.explanation: The structure of benzene reveals that it has 3 C-C double bonds, hence it has three π bonds for each double bond that it makes. It also possesses 6 C-C single bonds and 6 C-H single bonds, making a total of 12 σ bonds.Ratio of σ:π = 12:3 = 4:1Therefore option A is the correct answer. questions.text: The major source of unburnt hydrocarbons in the atmosphere is/are:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Petroleum is a fossil fuel composed of hydrocarbons and is used as a source of energy. It is refined to produce various fuels such as gasoline, diesel, and jet fuel. When petroleum-based fuels are burned, hydrocarbons can be released into the atmosphere. However, petroleum itself is not the major source of unburnt hydrocarbons; it is the burning of petroleum fuels that contributes to the release of hydrocarbons.

Natural gas is another fossil fuel composed mainly of methane, which is a hydrocarbon. Like petroleum, the burning of natural gas can release hydrocarbons into the atmosphere. However, natural gas is generally considered to have lower emissions compared to other fossil fuels, such as coal and oil. Therefore, while natural gas can contribute to hydrocarbon emissions, it is not the major source of unburnt hydrocarbons.

Automobiles, especially those powered by internal combustion engines, are a significant source of unburnt hydrocarbons in the atmosphere. When fuel is burned in car engines, incomplete combustion can occur, leading to the release of unburnt hydrocarbons into the air. This is especially true for older vehicles or those with poorly maintained engines and exhaust systems. In recent years, efforts have been made to reduce automobile emissions and improve engine efficiency to minimize the release of unburnt hydrocarbons.

While human beings do contribute to hydrocarbon emissions through activities such as cooking, heating, and the use of certain products like solvents and aerosols, the emissions from these sources are typically much lower compared to the emissions from automobiles and industrial processes. Therefore, while human activities can release hydrocarbons into the atmosphere, they are not the major source of unburnt hydrocarbons.

questions.explanation: There are many sources of unburned hydrocarbons. Unburned hydrocarbons (UHC) are typically an output of incomplete combustion due to unfavorable engine design, low fuel quality, or failure in the control system. Sometimes unburned hydrocarbons are released into the air during incomplete combustion. Burning fossil fuels, including gasoline in automobile engines, releases some hydrocarbons into the air. In a typical urban environment, the atmospheric concentration of hydrocarbons is around 3 ppm (parts per million).

questions.explanation: All of the compounds contain a single covalent bond between carbon and hydrogen atoms. Only that C2H2 and C2H4 contain triple and double bonds between carbon atoms respectively.

questions.text: Which of the following acts as an electrophile in the electrophilic substitution of benzene with bromine?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.explanation: Benzene reacts with bromine in an electrophilic substitution reaction, but only in the presence of a catalyst to yield bromobenzene. As a bromine molecule approaches the benzene ring, the delocalised electrons in the ring repel electrons in the bromine-bromine bond. It is the slightly positive end of the bromine molecule which acts as the electrophile. The presence of the aluminium chloride helps this polarisation.

questions.text: Catalytic hydrogenation of alkenes exhibits:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.explanation: Catalytic hydrogenation of alkenes exhibits addition reactions. Addition reactions in an unsaturated compound mean a compound that has a double bond can undergo addition reactions. The addition reactions can be classified into three main categories. Electrophilic addition: In these types of reactions, a species, that accepts an electron pair, attacks the electron-rich site of the double bond between carbon atoms. The reaction examples include the reactions of alkenes with hydrogen halides to give alkyl halides. Nucleophilic addition: In these types of reactions, species that have electron pairs, donate to form a chemical bond. These reactions usually occur in compounds having a carbonyl group like aldehydes or ketones. Free-radical addition: In these types of reactions, non-polar molecules form bonds with unsaturated compounds. These reactions occur when free radicals are formed. Free radicals are species that have unpaired valence electrons.
